prompt: This React component re-renders infinitely. What is the bug?
options: useState cannot be used with useEffect
The dependency array [count] causes the effect to run every time count changes, which itself changes count — infinite loop
setCount should use the functional form setCount(c => c + 1)
useEffect cannot return JSX
correct_answer: The dependency array [count] causes the effect to run every time count changes, which itself changes count — infinite loop
explanation: The effect runs when `count` changes, calls `setCount` which changes `count`, which triggers the effect again — infinite loop. If you want to run something once on mount, use an empty dependency array `[]`. If you need to increment on some event, move `setCount` into the event handler instead.

prompt: Your CI pipeline runs tests on every push and takes 20 minutes. PRs are blocked until tests pass. What is the best strategy to speed this up?
options: Skip tests on feature branches and only run them on main
Parallelise test suites across multiple CI workers and cache dependencies
Run tests only once a day in a scheduled job
Increase the timeout so slow tests do not fail
correct_answer: Parallelise test suites across multiple CI workers and cache dependencies
explanation: Splitting tests across parallel workers directly reduces wall-clock time. Caching dependencies (npm packages, Docker layers) avoids re-downloading them on every run. Skipping tests on feature branches defeats the purpose of CI. Scheduled jobs create long feedback loops.

prompt: A REST API endpoint creates a user and returns the new user. Which HTTP status code is most appropriate for a successful creation?
options: 200 OK
201 Created
204 No Content
202 Accepted
explanation: 201 Created specifically signals that a new resource was created as a result of the request. It should include a `Location` header pointing to the new resource. 200 is for successful requests that return existing data. 204 means success with no response body. 202 means the request was accepted but processing is not complete.

prompt: What is the output of this Python code?
options: TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
ValueError
correct_answer: TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
explanation: Tuples in Python are immutable — you cannot change, add, or remove elements after creation. To modify a tuple you must create a new one. This is different from lists, which are mutable.

prompt: You are reviewing a PR with 47 files changed and 2,000 lines of code. What is the most important feedback to give first?
options: Request style fixes and minor naming improvements before anything else
Ask the author to split it into smaller, focused PRs that are easier to review safely
Approve it quickly to unblock the team
Reject it and ask them to rewrite from scratch
correct_answer: Ask the author to split it into smaller, focused PRs that are easier to review safely
explanation: Large PRs are hard to review thoroughly — reviewers lose focus, miss bugs, and just approve to unblock. PRs under ~400 lines get meaningful review. Asking for smaller, logically separated PRs is the highest-value feedback you can give. Style issues are secondary to reviewability.

prompt: You want an LLM to extract all dates from a block of text and return them as a JSON array. Which prompt strategy is most reliable?
options: Ask the model to 'find dates' with no further instruction
Provide the exact output schema and a few-shot example with input and expected JSON output
Ask the model to first summarise the text, then extract dates
Increase the temperature to make the model more creative in finding dates
correct_answer: Provide the exact output schema and a few-shot example with input and expected JSON output
explanation: For structured extraction, specifying the exact schema (field names, types, format) and providing one or two examples dramatically reduces format errors. Some providers also offer JSON mode or function calling which further constrain the output. Vague instructions lead to inconsistent output that is hard to parse programmatically.

prompt: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array (not a linked list)?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n²)
explanation: Inserting at the beginning of an array requires shifting every existing element one position to the right to make room. With n elements, that is n shift operations — O(n). Linked list insertion at the head is O(1) since only pointer changes are needed.
